items.final_wording: Supports documenting history, examination, timing after exposure, erythema, urticaria, angioedema, respiratory or cardiovascular features, macules, papules, fixed eruption, fever, lymphadenopathy, mucosal erosion, vesicles, blistering, detachment, pustules, photosensitivity, and vasculitic features without asserting a diagnosis.

source.title: Drug allergy: diagnosis and management — Recommendations
source.jurisdiction: United Kingdom; international evidence requiring UAE allergy, emergency, medicine, and referral adaptation
source.population: People of all ages with a possible or established drug allergy or suspected drug-related reaction.
source.setting: Primary, emergency, inpatient, outpatient, and specialist-allergy documentation.
source.exclusions: Exact for documenting suspected-drug identity, indication, dose-count or exposure duration, route, reaction description, severity, timing, examination, and structured allergy record. It does not diagnose drug allergy, stop a medicine, generate treatment, or make a referral.

exact_location.heading: Recommendation 1.1.1 and Tables 1–3 — assessment, reaction patterns, and timing

source.evidence_paraphrase: Supports documenting history, examination, timing after exposure, erythema, urticaria, angioedema, respiratory or cardiovascular features, macules, papules, fixed eruption, fever, lymphadenopathy, mucosal erosion, vesicles, blistering, detachment, pustules, photosensitivity, and vasculitic features without asserting a diagnosis.

items.rationale: The exact NICE drug-allergy section supports documenting chronology, associated features, severity and relevant negatives when a medicine exposure is suspected; it does not establish allergy or generalize to every allergen.

items.final_wording: Supports structured recording of drug name, reaction signs, symptoms, severity, date and time, indication, number of doses or days before onset, route, status, and specialist-investigation results already available.

source.title: Drug allergy: diagnosis and management — Recommendations
source.jurisdiction: United Kingdom; international evidence requiring UAE allergy, emergency, medicine, and referral adaptation
source.population: People of all ages with a possible or established drug allergy or suspected drug-related reaction.
source.setting: Primary, emergency, inpatient, outpatient, and specialist-allergy documentation.
source.exclusions: Exact for documenting suspected-drug identity, indication, dose-count or exposure duration, route, reaction description, severity, timing, examination, and structured allergy record. It does not diagnose drug allergy, stop a medicine, generate treatment, or make a referral.

exact_location.heading: Recommendations 1.2.1–1.2.9 — recording and sharing drug-allergy information

source.evidence_paraphrase: Supports structured recording of drug name, reaction signs, symptoms, severity, date and time, indication, number of doses or days before onset, route, status, and specialist-investigation results already available.

items.rationale: The exact structured-record section supports a clinician-entered record of the reaction and plan, without automatically labelling an allergy or recommending treatment.

items.final_wording: Recognises rapid symptoms with airway, breathing, or circulation problems and usually skin or mucosal changes such as urticaria or angioedema; skin changes alone are not anaphylaxis.

source.title: Emergency treatment of anaphylaxis: Guidelines for healthcare providers
source.population: Adults and children with suspected anaphylaxis.
source.exclusions: Used only to support anaphylaxis warning documentation associated with rash or urticaria; it does not convert isolated skin findings into anaphylaxis.
source.evidence_paraphrase: Recognises rapid symptoms with airway, breathing, or circulation problems and usually skin or mucosal changes such as urticaria or angioedema; skin changes alone are not anaphylaxis.

items.rationale: Anaphylaxis recognition was reviewed to delimit severe airway, breathing or circulation presentations; it does not convert this broad allergic-reaction workflow into an anaphylaxis diagnosis.
